coredata.dc:title: Carbon Dot grafted pH sensitive smart paper for highly efficient separation of anionic/cationic dyes from a mixture
coredata.dc:creator: Bhattacharjee, Tuhin
Rabha, Rita
Chowdhury, Devasish
Majumdar, Gitanjali
coredata.dc:description: In the presented work, for the first time, a cheap green pH sensitive smart paper has been developed that contains in-situ generated carbon dots deposited uniformly over the fibrous surface of the paper via a one-step method from chitosan gel. The polymer carbon dot paper (PCD) has been characterized using Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM), Energy Dispersive X-Ray (EDX), Fourier Transformed Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Raman Microscopy, UV-Vis spectroscopy, Fluorescence spectroscopy, Thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA), Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS), Zeta Potential Measurements, Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM), Laser Confocal Microscopy (LCSM), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), and X-ray photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). The paper has been demonstrated to have very high selectivity for the absorptive removal of anionic dye from a mixture of cationic and anionic dyes in both acidic and neutral pH conditions. However, under basic conditions, the paper is found to be selective for the adsorption of Methylene Blue from a mixture of dyes. The dye separation experiment was carried out in an aqueous phase that was analyzed using UV–Visible spectrophotometer at a definite time interval and above 98% of the separation has been achieved working with a pair of various dye mixtures. The adsorbed dye from the paper can also be recovered very easily simply by agitating and tuning the pH conditions to basic up to the extent of 76%. The probable mechanism of the system in the separation of cationic and anionic dye is also discussed in the manuscript.

head.ce:title: Carbon Dot grafted pH sensitive smart paper for highly efficient separation of anionic/cationic dyes from a mixture

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the presented work, for the first time, a cheap green pH sensitive smart paper has been developed that contains in-situ generated carbon dots deposited uniformly over the fibrous surface of the paper via a one-step method from chitosan gel. The polymer carbon dot paper (PCD) has been characterized using Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM), Energy Dispersive X-Ray (EDX), Fourier Transformed Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Raman Microscopy, UV-Vis spectroscopy, Fluorescence spectroscopy, Thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA), Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS), Zeta Potential Measurements, Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM), Laser Confocal Microscopy (LCSM), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), and X-ray photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). The paper has been demonstrated to have very high selectivity for the absorptive removal of anionic dye from a mixture of cationic and anionic dyes in both acidic and neutral pH conditions. However, under basic conditions, the paper is found to be selective for the adsorption of Methylene Blue from a mixture of dyes. The dye separation experiment was carried out in an aqueous phase that was analyzed using UV–Visible spectrophotometer at a definite time interval and above 98% of the separation has been achieved working with a pair of various dye mixtures. The adsorbed dye from the paper can also be recovered very easily simply by agitating and tuning the pH conditions to basic up to the extent of 76%. The probable mechanism of the system in the separation of cationic and anionic dye is also discussed in the manuscript.

Novelty: For the first time, a smart pH sensitive carbon dot paper for separation of dyes from mixture is demonstrated.
Unlabelled Image

ce:sections.ce:para: In the presented work, for the first time, a cheap green pH-sensitive smart paper has been developed that contains in-situ generated carbon dots deposited uniformly over the fibrous surface of the paper via a one-step method from chitosan gel. The polymer carbon dot paper (PCD) has been characterized using Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM), Energy Dispersive X-Ray (EDX), Fourier Transformed Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Raman Microscopy, UV-Vis spectroscopy, Fluorescence spectroscopy, Thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA), Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS), Zeta Potential Measurements, Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM), Laser Confocal Microscopy (LCSM), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), and X-ray photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). The paper has been demonstrated to have very high selectivity for the absorptive removal of anionic dye from a mixture of cationic and anionic dyes in both acidic and neutral pH conditions. However, under basic conditions, the paper is found to be selective for the adsorption of Methylene Blue from a mixture of dyes. The dye separation experiment was carried out in an aqueous phase that was analyzed using a UV‐Visible spectrophotometer at a definite time interval and above 98% of the separation has been achieved working with a pair of various dye mixtures. The adsorbed dye from the paper can also be recovered very easily simply by agitating and tuning the pH conditions to basic up to the extent of 76%. The probable mechanism of the system in the separation of cationic and anionic dye is also discussed in the manuscript.

ce:section.ce:para: The world without dyes is unthinkable due to its wide variety of applications starring in textile, cosmetics, solar cells, biomarkers, contrast agents, etc. But because of its acute toxicity, it is of utmost necessity to ameliorate the environment from these harmful chemicals. Currently, a large amount of discharge of these used dyes in water occurs from industries related to textile, paper, paint, leather, food, cosmetics, etc. These discharges are increasing at an alarming rate concerning their ever-increasing demand for various commodity products. Typically, many of these dyes are quite stable in the environment even at various pH, in presence of light, heat, and against various oxidizing and reducing agents. With non-biodegradability and toxicity like carcinogenic [1] or mutagenic property [2] exhibited by many synthetic dyes, removal of dyes from various water bodies has become one of the recent thrust areas in environmental sciences.

To tackle and contain the toxicity posed by dye-based materials, many techniques have been demonstrated that can effectively remove dyes from wastewater for their purification. The water purification techniques involve adsorption, degradation involving oxidation, ion exchange, chemical precipitation, coagulation-flocculation, electrochemical treatment, membrane filtration, biological technique, hybrid process, and sonication [3–5]. Among several types of remediation strategies, adsorption and photodegradation of dyes are among the most preferred strategies for the elimination of dyes from the environment. However, photodegradation of dyes requires a catalyst usually with a sacrificial reagent for the conversion of dyes to non-toxic components. The advantage of adsorption-based remediation is that it does not require any such specific chemicals and generally takes place in normal conditions. This makes adsorption-based remediation techniques more eco-friendly, cost-effective, and practical. Given such advantages over the years, many new adsorbents have been developed by various synthetic strategies having fascinating properties, including interesting morphologies, high adsorption efficiency, and selectivity. The materials that have been used for adsorption of contaminants include nanoparticles [6–8], nanotubes [9–12], nanorods [13–15], nanowires [16,17], 2D nanosheets [18–21], zeolites [22–23], metal-organic frameworks [24,25], covalent organic frameworks [26], activated carbon [27] and various biomass [28]. Such materials provide scope for further modifications of this system with interesting properties and better results.

In the current scenario, most such adsorbents purify water by a simple adsorption approach that show's low selectivity. It is a great challenge for researchers to separate two types of pollutants based on selective adsorption. With the advancement of material science research, efforts must be given to the development of separation technologies for the mixtures of dyes, along with the reusability of the adsorbed materials leading to sustainability and lesser environmental toxicity in the long run. Dyes are used in many biological and analytical testing usually in combination [29], which are discarded after single usage. This may lead to increased pollution as well as wastage of the costly dye. If the components could be separately adsorbed, then they could be stored for later usage ensuring greater sustainability. Research groups are currently investigating the selective separation of dyes using chemically engineered adsorbents employing both organic, inorganic, nanostructured materials and smartly designed hybrid materials. Recently, Zhao et al [30] used porous boron nitride nano/microrods with selective adsorption toward cationic dye from the mixture. Mohammad et al [31] employed polydopamine and melamine–formaldehyde coated cellulose nanocrystals for selective adsorption of methylene blue from a mixture with anionic dyes. Modified amorphous silica synthesized by Shen et al [32] was used to separate cationic/anionic tritoluene dyes from their mixture. In addition, 2D porous MOF was used by Li et al [33] to selectively adsorb methylene blue and rhodamine B.

A detailed investigation of such adsorptive separation establishes the role of several fundamentally important interactions. For example, encapsulations of a lamellar polysilicate magadiite in chitosan were incorporated and the mechanism of adsorption of cationic and anionic dye was evaluated based on electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bonding interactions. It was observed that composite beads have a higher adsorption capacity for anionic dye than cationic dye [34]. Magnetic zeolite imidazolate framework (MZIF-67) was reported as a selective adsorbent for two types of anionic dye D8–86 and MO based on hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interaction [35]. Again, fibrous chitosan/sodium alginate foams were also found to be amphoteric adsorbent for both acid black 172 and Methylene Blue. Also, the composite was found to show tunable adsorption capacity depending on the mass ratio of chitosan to alginate [36]. Nanofibrous metal-organic framework is also reported to be a selective adsorbent for cationic dye from an aqueous solution. The adsorption capacity of the filter toward MB+ is found to be stable for five cycles [37]. Similarly, Zheng et all, have reported the construction of an amorphous metal-organic Pd12I24 cage namely alpha MOC-I for the rapid and time-dependent separation of dye [38]. For Ce (III) doped Zr-based metal-organic framework adsorption capacity of Ce(III) doped UiO-67 was found to be much better than UiO-67 due to the dominance of various adsorption forces [39].

Polysaccharides are a class of highly abundant green, sustainable polymers which may be linear like Chitosan, cellulose, carrageenan, etc., or branched as in guar gum, gum arabic, gum tragacanth etc. [40] The polysaccharides also have functional moieties like amino, sulphonyl, and phosphates which have been used advantageously for various applications. Chitosan is one such linear polysaccharide that is prepared by deacetylation of chitin extracted from shrimps. Chitosan has anime functionality which makes it a positively charged biopolymer and makes it valuable for various applications, such as in drug delivery [41], bio-adhesive [42], tissue engineering [43], adsorbent [44], food industry [45], composites [46], sensors [47], etc. In this work, we have attempted to use chitosan polysaccharide for the separation of a mixture of positive and negative dyes based on the fact that it has plenty of ionizable amino groups, which could be easily protonated and deprotonated by simply change of the pH. We have assembled the chitosan gel into dot-like structures on the paper in a singular step at moderate temperature. The dots-like structure shows excitation-dependent emission, a characteristic property shown by carbon dots. This carbon dot-like structure in the cellulose matrix will increase the surface-to-volume ratio and thereby enhance the overall chitosan activity. As chitosan is a positively charged polysaccharide, we have attempted to selectively separate anionic dye from a mixture of cationic and anionic dye. We have also tuned the charged condition of carbon dots by changing the pH using acid like hydrochloric acid, which shows similar results for anionic selectivity for dye as in neutral pH condition while using a base like sodium hydroxide, no adsorption of anionic dye was observed. Rather the paper becomes selective for methylene blue in a mixture with anionic dyes like eosin yellow and methyl orange. In addition, by tuning the pH to basic with slight agitation, it was possible to recover up to 76% of the adsorbed anionic dye from the carbon dot paper.

In the general context, most of the reported materials so far, use the hectic multistep synthetic procedure for adsorbents in form of powder [30–33] or composite [48] which inhibits their scalability or affordability for cost-effective large-scale separation. Furthermore, most solid powder-based systems are difficult to handle and need specialized techniques for the separation of the powders after adsorption. Herein, we have reported a simple one-step method for the synthesis of carbon dots from chitosan gel directly on the filter paper for highly efficient dye separation making it a greener and low-cost method with greater sustainability.

The used chemicals and reagents such as Chitosan (SRL, Low molecular weight extra pure), Whatman filter paper No 42 (125 mm), acetic acid, double distilled water, acetone, methylene blue, methyl orange, eosin yellow, murexide, fuchsin Basic, and rhodamine B were of laboratory grade and purchased from Merck.

About 0.5 g of chitosan powder was weighted and mixed with 25 mL of 1% acetic acid solution in 100mL beaker at normal room temperature. The above mixture was then stirred in a magnetic stirrer for 3–4 h until the complete dissolution of chitosan to form a viscous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chitosan CD grafted on filter paper (PCD)
ce:section.ce:para: The Whatman filter paper No 42 was cut in the required size (2*2 cm2) and pre-treated by dipping in 1% acetic acid solution for about 1 min. The paper was flipped after a 30-s interval for uniform coating. Now, the filter papers were dipped in 2% chitosan solution for 1 min (30 s for each side) and transferred to a Petri plate, and heated in a hot air oven at a temperature of 140 °C for nearly 2 h leading to the formation of brownish coloration of the filter paper indicating the formation of carbon dots.
The molecular properties of the dyes and the amount required to prepare 0.1 mM dye stock solution are shown in Table 1 .

The prepared dye stock solution was then further diluted to a 5 μM concentration for the adsorption experiment by taking 0.5 mL of dye from the stock solution and making up the solution to 10 mL in a volumetric flask.

The morphology of the synthesized system and Energy Dispersive X-Ray spectra were acquired using Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM) using Zeiss, Gemini 500. Solid-state UV–Vis spectra was taken in Shimadzu, UV 2600. The fluorescence spectra were acquired using a spectrofluorometer (Horiba fluoromax-4c). The bond stretching frequencies of the PCD system before and after dye adsorption was taken using Fourier Transformed Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) in attenuated total reflection mode (ATR) in the range 500–4000 cm−1 using Spectrum Series, Perkin Elmer. Raman spectra were obtained using the Horiba Jobin Vyon model LabRam Micro-Raman instrument. The thermal degradation and stability of the normal paper and PCD were investigated using a Thermogravimetric analyzer (Perkin Elmer 4000) in the range of 35 °C to 800 °C. The adsorption and separation experiments were recorded using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (LABTRONICS MODEL LT-2201). The confocal fluorescence microscope image was taken using fluorescent microscope model LEICA DMI 3000 B. Atomic Force microscope (AFM) was done using NTEGRA-Vita from ND-MDT. XRD measurement was acquired with a powder XRD diffractometer (Bruker D8 ADVANCE ECO P-XRD). The crystallinity of normal paper and PCD was calculated to measure changes in crystallinity and amorphous nature after the formation of polymeric carbon dots on paper via the crystallinity index [49] formula stated below (eq. (1)),

XPS data were recorded using the Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB Xi + instrument. The carbon dots were extracted from PCD using Probe sonicator (PCI PKS-500FL).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption study of individual dyes (Contact Time)
ce:section.ce:para: For the contact time experiment of dyes, the experiments were carried out with a 5 μM concentration of three anionic dyes namely eosin yellow, methyl orange, and murexide, and with methylene blue as a cationic dye in the volume of 10 mL. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded over 24 h, at 0 min, 15 min, 30 min, 45 min, 60 min, 120 min, and 24 h as shown in the supplementary Fig. S1 and Fig. S2 along with their decrement from their respective dye solutions represented in histograms.

To further confirm, the selectivity of dyes, adsorption experiments with two other cationic dyes, namely Fuchsin basic and Rhodamine B with PCD system were recorded in UV–Vis spectra initially and after 24 h immersion of the PCD system. To further confirm that blank filter paper does not play any role in the absorption of the anionic dye, a filter paper of 2*2 cm2 size (Whatman no. 40) was immersed in eosin yellow (5 μM) solution of 10 mL and the UV–Vis spectra were recorded after 24 h to verify for any adsorption of eosin yellow in the paper. The absorption spectra (UV-VIS spectra) were taken for all dye solutions before the experiment at 0 min and after 24 h.

To verify the selectivity of the paper exclusively for anionic dye from a mixture of cationic dye and anionic dye, the following combinations of the dyes were chosen (1) Eosin yellow + Methylene Blue (EY + MB) and (2) Methyl orange + Methylene Blue (MO + MB). To establish the selective separation of the dyes, absorption spectra were recorded at the time interval of 0 min, 30 min, 60 min,90 min, 120 min, and 24 h.

The kinetics of the adsorption also plays a very important role in determining the efficiency. The pseudo-first order [51] and second order [51] kinetics respectively of the adsorption experiment were calculated using the formulae (eqs. (2)–(3)),

where qe (mg g−1) and qt (mg g−1) are the adsorptions of the dye at equilibrium and at any time ‘t’ (min), respectively, k1(min−1) is the pseudo first-order rate constant and k2 (g mg−1 min−1) is the pseudo second-order rate constant.

Various combinations of anionic and cationic dyes were mixed by taking 5 mL each of cationic and anionic dyes of 5 μM concentration collectively in a beaker. A 2 × 2 cm2 carbon dot paper was dipped in dye mixture solution and the UV–Visible spectra were taken for 24 h at various time intervals. In the experiment, much emphasis has been laid on MO + MB and EY + MB, as a model for adsorption-based separation of dyes and further discussed in the manuscript. Apart from the above-mentioned dyes, separation of cationic and anionic dyes with other combinations like FB + MO, FB + EY, RhB + MO, and RhB + EY with only 0 min and 24 h UV–Vis spectrum were recorded as shown in figures in the supplementary section along with their visual pictures before separation and after separation. The schematic of the experimental preparation of PCD and separation of anionic and cationic dye has been depicted in Fig. 1 .

To evaluate the performance of PCD in the separation of cationic and anionic dye from the mixture, an important parameter is separation efficiency, which is calculated using eq. (4) for anionic dye [50].

where α(anionic dye) is the separation efficiency of anionic dye, Ct (anionic dye) is the remaining concentration of cationic dye and Ct(cationic dye) is the remaining concentration of anionic dye.

To investigate the effect of pH in the separation process, the adsorption experiment was carried out in a broad pH range from 3 to 6 for acidic (3–5)/neutral pH (6) at the same time 8to 12 for basic pH. The dye stock solution was first prepared by dissolving the appropriate amount of dye of 0.1 mM (mentioned in Table 1) in the required pH solution. After preparation of the dye stock solution of appropriate pH, 0.5 mL of stock solution was taken in a 10 mL volumetric flask and diluted with the same pH solution to get a 5 μM solution of dye at the specific pH mentioned above. The pH was adjusted with either HCl or NaOH as per required pH.

To observe the separation of dye in different pH conditions, 5 mL of cationic dye and 5 mL of anionic dye were mixed to get an overall 10 mL solution. PCD was dipped in this mixture and the changes were recorded using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

To find the adsorption capability of the dyes, we have tried to establish the adsorption isotherm with few modifications for two anionic dyes, eosin yellow, and methyl orange. As the paper by itself plays no role in adsorption or separation of a mixture of dyes, calculation using general Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm may lead to erroneous results due to the extra mass of paper itself. Furthermore, as the carbon dot is formed on the surface of the paper, herein the maximum absorption is calculated as mg/cm2 (QA). Thus, Langmuir [52] (eq. (5)) and Freundlich [18–53] (eq. (6)) isotherms along with the parameter Q were modified to take the amount adsorbed per unit surface area in mg/cm2 (QA) rather than the amount adsorbed per unit mass in mg g−1 (QM) in the formula.

where V is the volume of adsorbate solution, A is the surface area of the adsorbate, Co is the initial concentration (mg L−1) of the adsorbate and Ce is the equilibrium concentration (mg L−1) of the adsorbate.

where QMA (mg/cm2) is the maximum uptake of adsorbate per unit area, kaL (L mg−1) is the modified Langmuir adsorption equilibrium constant, kaF (L mg−1) is the modified Freundlich constant representing the adsorption capacity, n is a dimensionless constant depicting the intensity of adsorption.
For the isotherm and to find the adsorption capability of the system, batch experiments of methyl orange and eosin yellow were carried out at 5 μM, 50 μM, 100 μM, 250 μM, and 500 μM at 20 °C.

To recover the selectively adsorbed anionic dye on PCD, the PCD adsorbed with dye was immersed in 10 mL of 0.5 M NaOH solution and agitated in a shaker. The desorption of dyes from paper in a basic solution was recorded after 1 h using a UV–visible spectrophotometer.

In this work, we have attempted to fabricate a cheap paper-based system for the separation of dyes. For this purpose, we have synthesized carbon dots directly on paper for maximizing the utilization of enormous surface area of nanoparticles for the better adsorptive capability of chitosan in the form of nanodots. The results from the experiment using carbon dot grafted paper and normal paper are discussed below.

The optical properties of ordinary filter paper and PCD grafted filter paper were investigated using a UV–visible spectrophotometer and are depicted in Fig. 2A. Normal paper did not show any characteristic absorbance peak while the PCD grafted filter paper system absorb light in UV region with characteristic peaks at 261 nm and 293 nm which is due to π-π⁎ and n- π⁎ due to CN and CO functional groups of chitosan [54].

To verify the formation of carbon dots over the paper, fluorescence spectra were taken. Carbon dots in general show excitation-dependent fluorescence and PCD grafted filter paper is also found to show fluorescence which is dependent on the excitation wavelength and shift of 64 nm as shown in Fig. 2B which verified the formation of carbon dots over the paper. The PCD grafted filter paper under a UV lamp at 365 nm shows bluish-green coloration on the paper. The fluorescent colour also demonstrates the successful grafting of carbon dots on normal filter paper.

The temperature-dependent stability of normal filter paper and PCD grafted filter paper was conducted (Fig. 2C) that initially showed that normal filter paper had better stability with 8.4% weight loss at 313 °C and PCD with nearly 13% weight loss at 306 °C. After this temperature, there is a steep loss of weight signifying low thermal stability till 375.99 °C for normal paper and 371.8 °C for PCD which is possibly due to the degradation of oxygenated groups present in both cellulose and chitosan carbon dots. Following this temperature, there is a steady decline in thermal stability with better performance of PCD grafted filter paper over normal filter paper, with nearly 97% weight loss of PCD grafted filter paper at 800 °C, however, normal paper degrades over 99% at 697.95 °C.

The FTIR spectra of normal filter paper and PCD grafted filter paper are shown in Fig. 2D. The normal filter paper shows broadened peak at 3304 cm−1 (A1) corresponding to OH stretching of alcohol functionality which gets broadened due to H-bonding [55]. The normal filter paper also shows significant peaks at 2881 cm−1 (A2) and 1330 cm−1 (A5) because of CH stretching of methylene groups and OH bending respectively of alcoholic groups [56]. A very sharp peak is observed at 1023 cm−1 (A6) which is a combination of ether COCand alcoholic CO bond stretching. Few minor peaks at 1636 cm−1 (A3) and 1429 cm−1 (A5) were also observed due to OH bending of water [57] and CH bending of methylene group respectively. In PCD grafted filter paper similar peaks to that of normal filter paper were observed with some differences due to the presence of amino groups from carbon dots. A broadened intense peak was observed at 3278 cm−1 (B1) due to OH of alcohol and NH of amine moieties while another very noteworthy peak at 1006 cm−1 (B6) was due to the combination of bond stretching of COC of ether, CO of alcohols and CN bond of amine [58] (Fig. 2D). Other relatively significant peaks were observed at 2884 cm−1 (B2) and 1321 cm−1 (B5) due to the presence of CH bond stretching of methylene groups and OH bending of alcohols. A small NH bending peak from amine as well as an OH bending peak of deformed water bonds was observed at 1640 cm−1 (B3) and at the same time a small peak at 1429 cm−1 (B4) was seen due to CH bending of a methylene group.

Raman spectroscopy investigation was done for normal paper and PCD as shown in Fig. 2E. The peaks of normal paper and PCD were deconvoluted to find information on Raman bands, especially pertaining to those for the D band and G band (Fig. S3A and S3B). The peaks corresponding to the D band and G band are because of sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbon. For both normal paper and PCD, D and G bands were observed at 1317.17 cm−1 and 1544.55 cm−1. The D band intensities in both normal paper (ID = 1095.33) and PCD (ID = 1129.74) relatively remain the same while there is some increase in the intensity of the G band in PCD (IG = 884.55) to that of normal paper (IG = 613.15) by a margin of 271.4. This is due to some increase in sp2 carbon from carbon dots. Other Raman bands have been described in Table 2 [59,60].

The XRD diffractogram of normal filter paper and PCD grafted filter paper is shown in Fig. 2F. Normal filter shows sharp peaks at 15°, 16.63°, and 22.86° corresponding to the crystalline cellulosic domain. In our view the carbon dots deposited are amorphous in nature which could be regarded as intermediate between carbonized graphitic-core like carbon dot [58] and bulk chitosan, there is only a slight decrease in intensity of cellulose XRD peaks with minor shifts. To verify, that the amorphous polymeric carbon dots were deposited on the paper, the crystallinity index of normal paper and PCD was calculated. It was found that there is a decrease in crystallinity index from 78.35% to 73.54% respectively upon the formation of carbon dot on the cellulosic moiety.

To establish that the material grafted on the normal filter paper is indeed carbon dots, a Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) experiment was conducted by extracting the carbon dots from the PCD system. To extract the carbon dots, the carbon dot containing paper was kept in 1% acetic acid solution and agitated by sonication. The extracted solution was analyzed and the DLS results show a peak below 10 nm (Fig. 3A), which demonstrated the formation of carbon dot. However, it is to be noted that due to high surface area and in the absence of a stabilizer these polymeric carbon dots tend to aggregate quite fast in solution. In our case, the in-situ generated carbon dots are stabilized in the fibrous microstructure of the paper itself.

To investigate the surface morphology of normal filter paper and PCD, atomic force microscope analysis was done as shown in Fig. 3B & C respectively. In normal filter paper, rugged and uneven surfaces can be found. Upon modification of the paper with in-situ generated carbon dots, many small dots comprising of polymeric carbon dots [61] can be found indicated in black dashed circles in Fig. 3C. These carbon dots can be regarded as polymeric carbon dots because the temperature and pressure corresponding to carbon dot formation isn't high enough reported in the literature to form graphitic core like carbon dots [62–65].

The FESEM investigation of the normal paper and PCD smart paper was conducted as shown in Fig. 3D and E respectively. The image shown in Fig. 3E exhibited dot like structures spread uniformly across the fiber of cellulose. This indicates carbon dots grafted all over the paper using the thermal method.

Energy Dispersive X-ray Analysis was also done for both normal paper and PCD which are shown in the top right corner of Fig. 3D and F respectively. The EDX analysis shows the presence of nitrogen element in the PCD system, which was preliminary absent in the case of normal paper signifying the successful presence of carbon dot on paper from chitosan solution.

With the help of a confocal fluorescence microscope, the successful deposition of carbon dots on paper can also be confirmed. In the fluorescence microscope image in 10× magnification, the normal paper shows no emission as shown in Fig. 4A while PCD shows fluorescent emission as observed in Fig. 4B and C. Representative Confocal Fluorescence microscope images of PCD in (10×) and (63×) demonstrate the emission. The emission is obtained from the carbon dot grafted filter paper demonstrating the presence of carbon dots on filter paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption studies and interaction of the dye with PCD using FTIR
ce:section.ce:para: PCD was then investigated for the adoption of four dyes as mentioned in the experimental section which included eosin yellow, methyl orange, murexide, and methylene blue, each of 5 μM concentration. This resulted in appreciable adsorption for all anionic dyes with 98.63% of EY removal, 99.85% of MO removal, and 99.99% of MU removal however, this wasn't the case for MB which did not show any appreciable change with only 8.87% removal. The spectral changes with respect to time after adsorption on PCD and the histogram representation are shown in Supplementary Fig. S1 and Fig. S2.
The histogram of all dyes removal with PCD system and eosin yellow removal with normal filter paper as blank is shown in Fig. 4.

The pseudo kinetics of first order and the second order were calculated for MO and EY. The root means square value (R2) for the first order and second order for EY was 0.9739 and 0.9888 respectively while for EY was found to be 0.9859 and 0.9947 (Fig. 16A and 16B). The pseudo-first order rate constant and pseudo-second order rate constant respectively for EY is 0.0101 min−1 and 0.00057 g mg−1 min−1 respectively and for MO is found to 0.0128 min−1 and 0.01026 g mg−1 min−1 respectively (Fig. S4A–D).

After adsorption of EY and MO, FTIR spectra were recorded and are shown in Fig. 4E. FTIR spectra of PCD have been previously discussed and compared after adsorption of anionic dye, which indicated a few important changes. Newer peaks at 1161 cm−1 (Y6, Z6), 1110 cm−1 (Y7, Z7), and 1030 cm−1 (Y8, Z8) because of bond stretching of amino CN bonds, CO (2° alcohol) bonds and COC (ether)/CO (1° alcohol) bonds respectively. These newer peaks were the result of separation of the 1006 cm−1 peak of PCD because of the interaction of amino groups and, also maybe due to some H-bonding with alcoholic moieties with anionic dyes. Similarly, the NH bending peaks (Y3, Z3) were broadened under the influence of anionic dye.

For any system, recovery of adsorbate from adsorbent is important as it will demonstrate the efficiency and sustainability of the system. We have attempted to recover dye by tuning the pH to a basic medium for recovery of basic dyes. Within 1 h after immersion of dye PCD system in 0.5 M NaOH solution, a significant amount of dye was desorbed from the paper under agitation. In the current study, about 61.82% of EY was recovered from the paper and 76.32% of MO was recovered from paper (Fig. 4F).

As established in the dye adsorption experiment, the PCD system show selectivity for anionic dye. Hence, in the case of the EY + MB mixture, only EY was adsorbed with no change in concentration of MB. The change in spectra with time when PCD is dipped in the EY + MB mixture is shown in Fig. 5A. The interesting part of the experiment was that the separation can also be visualized with no such adsorption of MB occurring and the colour of the MB clearly visible and EY exclusively adsorbed (Fig. 5B). The spectra of separation of other combinations of dyes have been depicted in the supplementary section in Figs. S5–S7. Similarly in the MO + MB mixture, only MO was adsorbed with no change in concentration of MB. The change in spectra with time when PCD is dipped in the MO + MB mixture is shown in Fig. 5C. Here, too the separation can also be visualized with no such adsorption of MB occurring and the colour of the MB is visible in the solution and MO is exclusively adsorbed in PCD (Fig. 5D). The separation efficiency of the separation of dyes for EY + MB and MO + MB combinations were also evaluated. The separation efficiency was found to be 98.75% for the EY + MB dye mixture while for MO + MB mixture was found to be 99.40%.

The most fascinating aspect of this work is the effect of pH on selectivity in the separation of dyes as shown in Fig. 6 . The pH variation studies of the PCD system and its effect on selective dye adsorption-based separation were done over the pH range of 3–12 for EY + MB and MO + MB combinations of anionic and cationic dye mixtures (Fig. 6A and B). It was found that in acidic and neutral solutions, the PCD system had a greater affinity for anionic dye resulting in the separation of mixtures of dyes. As the pH was shifted to basic condition, there is a decrement in the adsorption of anionic dye while there is an increment in the adsorption of methylene blue in particular. However, the adsorption of anionic dye is highly dependent on the quaternary site available in the system, which decreases due to the abstraction of a proton from quaternary amine via the OH ion of a base. This is evident, especially in the case of MO + MB combination. Thus, the PCD system shows amphoteric like attributes depending on the pH [50].

To verify whether the selectivity of the system is for cationic dye or exclusively for MB, we conducted an individual test of adsorption of rhodamine B and fuchsin basic in PCD at 20 μM concentration at pH 12. It was observed that rhodamine B has a very low affinity for the PCD system even under basic condition after contact with the PCD system for 24 h with only 14.04% removal has been achieved. The spectral change is depicted in Fig. S8. However, a similar experiment could not be conducted for fuchsin basic as it shows instability at basic conditions leading to discoloration of the solution over time.

In pH 3 (acidic medium) and pH 12 (basic medium), the separation efficiency of anionic dyes was calculated for MO + MB and EY + MB combinations. In pH 3, the separation efficiency of anionic dye for MO + MB combination was 99.83% while for EY + MB combination was 99.94%. In pH 12, separation for MO + MB and EY + MB combination was found to be 6.01% and 8.63% respectively.

The modified isotherms, Langmuir and Freundlich were conducted in the concentration range 5 μM – 500 μM. In Langmuir isotherm, QMA and kaL for MO were found to be 0.19780 mg/cm2 and 0.17518 L mg−1 respectively, and in contrast, EY was found to be 0.57402 mg/cm2 and 0.01524 L mg−1 respectively. In Freundlich isotherm, the calculated value of n and kaF on adsorption of MO in PCD is 2.5533 and 0.040826 L mg−1 as for EY it is found to be 1.70363 and 0.0196 L mg−1 respectively. The modified Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm has been depicted in Fig. 7A–D.

The elemental analysis and binding energy characteristics of different bonds of the cellulosic systems comprising of normal paper, PCD, and also PCD after adsorption of eosin yellow on PCD were analyzed using X-ray Photoelectron spectroscopy. The survey of the above mentioned systems has been shown in Fig. 8A–C. The survey shows characteristics of N1s binding energy in PCD and PCD-EY at 400 eV which is a striking contrast to normal paper hinting toward successful deposition of polymeric carbon dots.

The C1s spectra of the three above mentioned systems are shown in Fig. 8D–E along with their deconvoluted peaks. The normal paper shows distinct binding energy peaks at 284.78 eV, 286.21 eV, and 287.92 eV corresponding to CC bonds, CO bonds, and OCO/CO bonds respectively [66,67]. In presence of polymeric carbon dots in the PCD system, the sharpest peak is found at 284.78 eV because of the CC bond which has an increase in intensity from 7021.22 to 25,774.67. This substantial increase in intensity can be attributed to new CC bonds of carbon dots. Other peaks of 286.21 eV and 287.92 eV were seen due to CO/CN bonds and OCO/CO/N-CO (amide) bonds respectively [66,67]. After adsorption of eosin yellow in PCD, deconvoluted peaks at 284.80 eV, 286.22 eV, and 287.52 eV due to CC bonds, CO/CN bonds, and OCO/CO/N-CO (amide)/C(O)OH (Carboxylic acid) bonds respectively. Due to adsorption of carboxylic acid moiety from eosin yellow dye, it is expected there to be an increase in the intensity of the corresponding peak at 287.52 and rightfully so, there has been an increase in intensity from 4247.47 of PCD (287.92 eV) to 6512.82 (287.57 eV) along with a minor shift.

The O1s XPS similar to C1s was also conducted to get information on the state of oxygenated species of normal paper, PCD, and PCD-EY shown in Fig. 9A–C. Deconvolution of normal paper results in a major peak at 533 eV and 533.40 eV which corresponded to OH of hydroxyl group and CO of ether functionality of cellulose [66]. A minor peak at 531.45 eV can be observed due to some CO species resulting from minor oxidation. In contrast, PCD, shows peaks with minor shift for CO for amide bonds, OH of hydroxyl groups, and CO of ether groups at 531.85 eV, 532.76 eV, and 533 eV respectively [67]. There is a decrease in intensity for hydroxyl groups from 47,999.48 in normal paper to 15,219.15 in PCD as well as for CO functionality from 8677.49 in normal paper to 6126.31 in PCD. This is due to the formation of carbon dots from chitosan which has relatively fewer oxygenated groups compared to cellulose. Similarly, there is a substantial increase in the intensity of CO groups from 1645.05 to 10,071.43 in PCD due to the formation of carbon dots on paper which contains amide groups from chitosan. Upon adsorption of eosin yellow on PCD, peaks at 531.64 eV, 532.75 eV, and 533.10 eV. These peaks corresponded to CO for amide bonds, OH of hydroxyl groups as well as carboxylate groups, and CO of ether groups.

To know about the states of nitrogen in PCD and PCD-EY, N1s spectra were recorded and are exhibited in Fig. 9D–E. The N1s of PCD show characteristics peaks after deconvolution at 399.02 eV, 399.65 eV ad 401.38 eV for CN of amine, CNHC(O) of amide, and CN+ of quaternary amine [68,69]. After adsorption of EY on PCD, the N1s spectrum shows similar peaks at 399.02 eV, 399.65 eV, and 401.58 eV due to CN of amine bonds, CNHC(O) of amide bonds, and CN+ of quaternary amine bonds. Upon adsorption of EY, a decrease in the magnitude of amine functionality from 1692.15 in PCD to 1450.89 in PCD-EY, while there is an increase in magnitude in quaternary amine from 397.02 to 691.22, can be observed. This is probably due to the transfer of a proton from the undissociated carboxylic acid group in EY to amine in PCD leading to a higher number of the quaternary amine group. This quaternary amine formed now electrostatically binds to EY. It is however, important to note, dissociated carboxylic acid group of EY directly binds to PCD electrostatically. Electrostatic interactions of EY with PCD were confirmed by extracted carbon dot from PCD and its interaction with EY during zeta potential analysis discussed below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic insight and zeta potential analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Thus, we have been able to establish the formation of carbon dots directly on cellulosic fibers and used it smartly to separate anionic and cationic dyes from their mixture. In this work, we investigated the system for simple paper-based separation, where the system works via electrostatic-based interactions. Keeping this view, Zeta potential analysis of the extracted carbon dot solution was done to get information about the surface charge of the polymeric carbon dots and its electrostatic interaction with anionic dye, namely eosin yellow as exhibited in Fig. 10A and Fig. 10B respectively. The zeta potential of the polymeric carbon dot was found to be +34.2 mV signifying the formation positively charged carbon dots with quaternary amine -NH3 + group grafted and scattered throughout the cellulose fibers at the surface. Upon the addition of eosin yellow, the zeta potential decreased to +14.6 mV. This showed the presence of oppositely charged interaction between positively charged carbon dots in PCD and negatively charged dye like eosin yellow. Similar interaction is expected with other anionic dyes leading to selective adsorption from a mixture of cationic and anionic dyes. This evidence supplements the observation found in FTIR and XPS analysis. In basic conditions, the quaternary amine group reverts to a simple –NH2 group due to which, there is no site for anionic dyes to hold onto the PCD system. This also explains the desorption of adsorbed anionic dye using NaOH solution with slight agitation. The methylene blue dye specifically binds to NH2 group and OH group in basic condition [70] rather than the NH3 + group present in acidic and neutral conditions. Hence, the system is found to be specific for methylene blue for basic conditions. The overall scheme of interaction is shown in Fig. 10C.

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, we have reported one-step method for the synthesis of carbon dots and also grafted it directly on the paper using a simple thermal method which was confirmed using FESEM, AFM, DLS and fluorescence spectra. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time a simple, cheap and environmentally benign smart paper has been demonstrated that is capable of separating anionic and cationic dyes from a mixture efficiently. This smart paper exhibited pH-based selectivity, in which anionic dye is adsorbed specifically on the system at neutral and acidic pH while in basic conditions, the smart paper showed affinity specifically for methylene blue. Furthermore, a large proportion of anionic dye adsorbed on the paper can be recovered by simply tuning to basic conditions via immersion of anionic dye paper in NaOH solution along with simple agitation. Such smart paper-based technology is expected to create more opportunities and progress in the separation and purification of a wide variety of analytes using such green and low-cost functional paper. The innovativeness and credibility of our method are that it is a very simple to prepare, sustainable and all green chemicals are used here. The commercial scalability and applicability are more viable using our method making it accessible to a broad audience in general.
